exemplar.title: To what extent has the changes in the water quality measured in terms of pH, BOD, DO and Conductivity in Sabarmati River affected its suitability for aquatic life from 2012 to 2021?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the changes in the water quality measured in terms of pH, BOD, DO and Conductivity in Sabarmati River affected its suitability for aquatic life from 2012 to 2021?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report is well structured and organized with clear headings and a table of contents
Logical progression from context to conclusion aids readability
weaknesses: Terminology usage is accurate but some sections are verbose and contain typos
Unit notation is inconsistent across tables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With band scores of 3 for structure but 2 for terminology/concision and 2 for logic/coherence, overall communication fits the mid band. Strength in organization is offset by language inconsistencies, warranting 2/3.

strengths: Research question is clear, focused and testable, specifying location, timeframe and variables
Introduction offers substantive, relevant context linking industrial growth and pandemic effects
Connections between environmental issue and research question are coherently explained
weaknesses: Context lacks quantitative pollution data to reinforce narrative
Minor omissions in linking dissolved oxygen stress explicitly in context
Some phrasing is wordy and could be more concise
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed two strands at band 5 and one at band 6, noting a highly coherent, focused question and detailed contextual discussion but with minor omissions (e.g., quantitative evidence, explicit DO link). These place performance solidly in the top band but shy of perfection, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Methodology is logically ordered and repeatable using publicly available data
Sampling strategy is described, capturing spatial and temporal variation
Risks and ethical considerations are acknowledged
weaknesses: Insufficient detail on statistical procedures (test type, software, formulae) for full reproducibility
Sampling strategy lacks justification of station representativeness or alternatives
Risk and ethical discussion is brief and no formal risk matrix or dataâlicensing issues are addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 4, 3 and 3 indicate a repeatable but underâjustified method with omitted procedural details and ethics discussion. This performance fits the mid of 3â4 band but closer to its lower range, so 3/6 is appropriate.

strengths: Discussion links degraded water parameters to ecological consequences and SDG relevance
Method strengths, weaknesses and limitations are identified with specific examples
Meaningful research extensions (biological data, seasonal variation) address study gaps
weaknesses: Evaluation remains qualitative without assessing the magnitude or impact of limitations
No prioritization of which methodological weaknesses most threaten validity
Proposed improvements lack methodological detail on implementation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands scored band 3, indicating a midâband performance with relevant but superficial evaluation and extensions. The discussion meets descriptors for 3â4 but does not exceed them, so 3/6 is justified.

strengths: All relevant data are presented in wellâlabelled tables and charts covering the study period
Statistical calculations are largely correct and interpreted appropriately
Visualizations allow clear comparison against standards
weaknesses: No acknowledgement of missing 2014 data point in tables
Charts lack error bars or confidence intervals to show variability
Inconsistencies in pâvalue criteria and omission of degrees of freedom undermine transparency
Conclusion overstates âcritically lowâ DO despite acceptable mean values
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation meets topâband criteria (band 6), but analysis and conclusion scoring at band 5 with notable errors and omissions drag the holistic performance down into the 3â4 band. A score of 4/6 reflects strong presentation but incomplete, occasionally flawed analysis and conclusion.

strengths: Solutions are clearly linked to measured waterâquality findings and well justified
Multiple concrete applications are proposed reflecting study outcomes
weaknesses: Evaluation of each solution omits key factors such as governance feasibility and monitoring requirements
Justifications lack specific metrics (e.g., buffer widths, treatment capacities)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band scores of 3 for proposal and 2 for evaluation place performance in the mid band. While the link to findings is strong, the incomplete evaluation of solution strengths and weaknesses merits 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialist terms (BOD, ÂµS cmâ»Â¹, t-test) are used correctly, yet several passages are verbose and contain minor typographical errors.  Hence the work is either concise or terminology-rich but not both simultaneously, fitting the 2-mark descriptor.

The discussion links degraded parameters to ecological consequences and regional SDG indicators, providing context for significance.  Nevertheless, evaluation is mostly qualitative and does not revisit the statistical confidence limits or alternative explanations, leaving some omissions; thus mid-band is appropriate.

The conclusion synthesises statistical outcomes with standards, explicitly answering the research question and quantifying the extent of deviation.  It is logically derived from the data, but slight over-generalisation (e.g., claiming âcritically lowâ DO when mean is acceptable) prevents awarding the very top mark.

Means, standard deviations and one-sample t-tests are calculated for each parameter; trends over time are described.  Calculations appear correct and p-values are interpreted appropriately.  However, the student does not show test statistics for every year nor examine relationships between variables, so the analysis is thorough but not entirely complete.

The student proposes adding biological population data and investigating seasonal variation, both of which address clear weaknesses.  While meaningful, these suggestions are limited in number and depth and do not outline how they would rectify methodological flaws comprehensively, hence a score of 3.

The six-step methodology explains data sourcing, averaging, statistical treatment and graphical display in a logical order.  Because the study relies on publicly available secondary data, replication is feasible with the information given.  However, sampling dates, software used and exact formulae for the t-test are not provided, so some detail is still lacking, placing it at the top of the 3-4 band.

Section 1.3 explicitly links each parameter to the stated environmental threats and justifies the 10-year window (including the COVID period).  The student explains how changes in industrial discharge, runoff and lockdowns could influence the variables, providing a coherent narrative of cause-and-effect.  Minor omissions (e.g., lack of explicit reference to dissolved oxygen stress despite later discussion) prevent a perfect score.

The student frames a clear, narrowly-defined question that specifies the location (Sabarmati River), temporal scale (2012-2021), water-quality variables (pH, BOD, DO, conductivity) and the criterion for judgement (suitability for aquatic life).  The wording avoids ambiguity and can be directly tested with the data set provided, demonstrating full focus and coherence.

The strengths and drawbacks of each solution are mentioned (cost, compliance, time).  Some relevant factorsâpolitical feasibility, monitoring effectivenessâare absent, so the evaluation is useful but incomplete, fitting the middle band.
A logical structure with numbered sections, headings and a table of contents is maintained throughout the report, enabling easy navigationâconsistent with the highest band for organisation.

Strengths, weaknesses and limitations are listed separately with specific examples (e.g., secondary data dependence, missing 2014).  Discussion remains brief and does not analyse magnitude of each limitation or their effect on validity, meeting the descriptor for 3-4 but not exceeding it.

The introduction and section 1.2 offer a substantive description of the Sabarmatiâs economic, cultural and ecological importance, outline the scale of industrial and sewage pollution, and explain why the four chosen parameters are critical to aquatic life.  Although mainly descriptive, the context is detailed and clearly relevant, meriting the top band, albeit with a minor lack of quantitative evidence that keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

The student identifies the approach as stratified (multiple stations) and temporal, and briefly states why these choices capture spatial and yearly variation.  This constitutes a description rather than a full justification (no discussion of station representativeness or alternative designs), fitting solidly in the 3-4 band but nearer the lower mark.

Risk is acknowledged (none for field safety, potential for data reliability) and ethical points cover citation, transparency and non-manipulation.  While all relevant aspects are touched upon, the discussion is brief and lacks a formal risk matrix or detailed data-ethics critique, so the strand meets but does not exceed the 3-4 descriptor.

Raw-data tables, processed-data tables and four comparative bar charts are included, covering every parameter for every year available.  Axes are labelled and standards are super-imposed, allowing immediate visual comparison.  Despite a missing 2014 data point, all relevant data that exist are presented clearly, satisfying the highest descriptor.

Four concrete solutions are proposed, each explicitly linked to the polluted parameters (e.g., STPs for BOD, industrial pretreatment for conductivity).  The rationale shows clear alignment with findings, fully satisfying the justification requirement for the top band.

Arguments generally flow from data to interpretation, but occasional leaps (e.g., conflating high BOD with acceptable DO) disrupt coherence.  These issues make some sections harder to follow, meeting the âmainly logical but difficult in partsâ descriptor.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is well organized with clear sectioning, facilitating easy navigation throughout the report.

The research question is clearly defined, specifying location, variables and timeframe, ensuring coherence and testability. Consider refining phrasing to reduce wordiness while retaining essential details.
The title captures the studyâs focus but could include the 2012â2021 temporal scope for added precision and immediate context.
The global introduction to aquatic wildlife is informative but generic. Strengthen context by linking these functions directly to the Sabarmati River ecosystem with local data or statistics.
The detailed description of the Sabarmatiâs cultural and economic importance is strong, but quantitative evidence (e.g., pollution load statistics) would enhance the environmental context.

The connection between industrial growth, pandemic lockdowns and water-quality trends is clearly explained. To deepen analysis, explicitly mention how each parameterâs biotic impact is anticipated to change.
The hypothesis predicts directional changes but uses vague phrasing for pH. State numerical thresholds (e.g., âpH will fall below 6.5â) to ensure specificity.
The controlled-variable justification lists consistent sources and standards but lacks detail on how consistency is maintained (e.g., calibration methods). Add procedural controls.
Averaging midpoints of reported ranges assumes uniform distribution. Justify this approach or discuss the potential bias introduced by non-uniform data.
The methodology mentions a one-sample t-test but omits whether itâs one- or two-tailed and the software or formula used. Include these details for full reproducibility.
The sampling strategy is described but not justified. Explain why stratified temporal sampling best captures both spatial variation and annual trends.
Ethical considerations list citation and transparency but omit discussion of data licensing or access permissions for CPCB data. Clarify any usage restrictions.
Table 1âs unit notation uses âÂµmho/cmâ but later tables use âÂµmhos/cmâ or âÂµS/cmâ. Standardize units to maintain clarity.
Processed data table headers omit the missing year 2014 without comment. Acknowledge gaps to contextualize temporal analysis.
Graph 1 lacks error bars or confidence intervals. Including these would visually convey variability and enhance data interpretation.
Graph 2 (BOD) does not indicate whether bars represent means, medians or other summaries. Clarify statistic displayed in caption or legend.
The description of pâvalue decision rules inconsistently uses p < 0.05 and p â¤ 0.05. Maintain consistency in hypothesisâtesting criteria.
Degrees of freedom and critical tâvalues are not provided in the sample calculation. Including df=9 and t_crit would strengthen statistical transparency.
Table 4 labels âNot Significantâ for pH despite p=0.00001; this contradicts your threshold. Review significance interpretation.
The BOD section correctly highlights organic pollution but lacks citation for classification thresholds. Add a reference for the âBOD>10 mg/L very poorâ criterion.
The DO discussion misinterprets significance: a significant tâtest indicates a difference from the standard, not necessarily suitability. Clarify the direction of effect.
Percentage calculations for conductivity exceedance are compelling but lack the formula and reference threshold values. Include calculation steps or citation.
The conclusion overstates âcritically lowâ DO despite mean DO being within acceptable limits. Align descriptors with analyzed data to avoid overgeneralization.
The discussion links results to SDGs effectively but would benefit from specific indicators or scores. Include quantitative SDG data to deepen evaluation.
The method evaluation lists strengths and weaknesses but lacks analysis of how each limitation affects validity and which is most critical.
Improvements section proposes adding biological data but lacks detail on how this would integrate methodologically. Outline sampling design and analysis for proposed extension.
The application proposals align well with findings. For afforestation, include potential planting density or riparian buffer dimensions to substantiate impact.
Solution evaluation mentions costs but omits monitoring and enforcement feasibility. Add factors such as governance capacity and community compliance.
The report is logically structured with clear headings and numbered sections, facilitating a coherent flow from context to conclusion.
Terminology is accurate but some sections are verbose. Aim for conciseness by eliminating redundant phrases (e.g., âIn spite of all its significanceâ).
Bibliography entries are comprehensive but not in alphabetical order. Arrange references per a consistent style guide to enhance readability.
